title: Social Psychology

jokes.text: And racist that i took the time personally to analyze that handwriting against past exams.
jokes.setup: Professor mentions harsh feedback on evaluation cards
jokes.punchline: Reveals he went full CSI handwriting analysis on anonymous feedback
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Duncan's petty vindictiveness and paranoia. The escalation from 'harsh feedback' to full forensic investigation is perfectly absurd and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: I received some rather harsh feedback on my evaluation cards. So I did what any reasonable academic would do—I performed a full CSI-level handwriting analysis to identify the anonymous critic.

jokes.text: And this particular coward dots her is with tiny, itty-bitty, little circles. Boop. Boop. Tcch! Isn't that precious and cute?
jokes.setup: Duncan's forensic handwriting analysis
jokes.punchline: Mockingly demonstrates the dotting with sound effects and condescending tone
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of the 'boop boop' demonstration combined with Duncan's seething passive-aggression creates a memorable character moment. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: You see, when analyzing handwriting, one must pay close attention to every minute detail. For instance, the way one dots their i's and crosses their t's reveals much about a person's character.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: *mockingly demonstrates dotting motions with exaggerated sound effects* Psh-psh-psh. Oh yes, very telling. A person who dots their i's with such careless abandon clearly has authority issues and probable substance abuse problems.

jokes.text: Who's 'erratic and unstable' now, Princess gringa?
jokes.setup: Duncan's entire rant about the evaluation
jokes.punchline: Asks who's unstable while being completely unhinged himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic self-awareness as Duncan demonstrates exactly the behavior he was criticized for. Great economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Who's unstable? Who's unstable? I'm perfectly stable! I have a very orderly life. I wake up, I evaluate people, I go to bed. That's stability. That's discipline. That's the mark of a well-adjusted individual. So don't you dare suggest that I'm unstable just because I occasionally raise my voice or organize my files by the molecular weight of their contents or spend fourteen hours recalibrating my grading scale because I detected a 0.003% variance in my assessment methodology. I am the picture of mental health! So I ask you: who here is unstable? Who?

jokes.text: I forgot to stagger the timing of my exit with shirley's.
jokes.setup: Jeff being asked if he forgot something
jokes.punchline: Reveals he strategically times his exits to avoid awkward small talk
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Jeff's calculated social maneuvering and mild sociopathy. The clinical phrasing makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I didn't forget anything. I strategically time my exits to avoid awkward small talk.

jokes.text: We both have a class across campus, and i can't go that kind of distance with 'ooh, that's nice.'
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining his exit timing strategy
jokes.punchline: Treats small talk stamina like athletic endurance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor treating conversation like a marathon. The specificity of Shirley's catchphrase and Jeff's social anxiety make this very character-specific and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You know, small talk is like a marathon. You can't just sprint out of the gate. You pace yourself, conserve energy, and then when you see your opening, you make your move.

jokes.text: Yeah, but i'm a sprinter. I'm at my best during high-speed bursts of wit.
jokes.setup: Britta challenging Jeff's conversational abilities
jokes.punchline: Extends the athletic metaphor to describe his wit as track and field
jokes.explanation: Great callback that extends the metaphor while revealing Jeff's self-aware vanity about his conversational skills.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Your conversational abilities aren't as sharp as you think they are.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Oh please, my wit is like track and field — it's fast, it's competitive, and I always finish strong.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: More like the long jump — lots of buildup, but you never quite stick the landing.

jokes.text: 'Cause i might be worried if i was playing hacky sack a decade too late.
jokes.setup: Vaughn saying 'no worries'
jokes.punchline: Immediately roasts him for playing hacky sack past its cultural expiration date
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback that establishes Jeff's disdain for Vaughn while being culturally specific about dated activities.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: No worries.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: No worries? Dude, hacky sack expired culturally in 1987. You're like three decades too late to the party.

jokes.text: All right, but i just wanted to point out that we easily walked more than 100 yards with no awkward pauses
jokes.setup: Jeff trying to prove he can have normal conversations
jokes.punchline: Quantifies successful social interaction like a scientific measurement
jokes.explanation: Jeff's clinical approach to friendship, measuring success in yards and pause-free intervals, shows his disconnection from normal social interaction.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I can have normal conversations. I'm very normal. See? I'm talking to you right now about regular things.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That was a successful social interaction. I'd rate it at about 7.3 millimeters of normal.

jokes.text: I'm really glad that you're not hitting on me anymore.
jokes.setup: Jeff trying to prove they're good friends
jokes.punchline: Britta immediately creates the exact awkward pause Jeff was trying to avoid
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and structure. The moment Jeff declares victory over awkward pauses, Britta creates one. Classic sitcom irony executed well.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We're good friends, Britta. I can prove it.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Name one time I've ever made things awkward between us.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: That.

jokes.text: There's the awkward pause.
jokes.setup: The awkward silence after Britta's comment
jokes.punchline: Jeff's defeated acknowledgment of his failed social experiment
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy in acknowledging the comedic beat. Jeff's deadpan delivery makes it funnier than it should be.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah, okay, I walked into that one.

jokes.text: But i had a 4.0 at riverside high, and i'm not looking down on this school at all, but i'm only here because of a brief addiction to pills that i was told would help me...focus, but they actually made me lose my scholarship and virginity.
jokes.setup: Annie trying to impress Professor Duncan
jokes.punchline: Escalates from academic credentials to deeply personal oversharing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Annie character moment - her overachieving nature leads to inappropriate oversharing. The escalation from 'brief addiction' to losing virginity is perfectly awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Professor Duncan, I just wanted to show you my extra credit essay on comparative literature. I stayed up all night researching. Also, I've won three academic awards this semester. And I'm fluent in Spanish, Mandarin, and conversational French. Oh, and I'm a member of the National Honor Society.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: My father never believed in me, so I've been trying to prove myself my whole life. I have trust issues. I bite my nails when I'm anxious, which is always. I once cried in a Bed Bath & Beyond because they were out of my preferred thread count. I'm terrified of disappointing people, which is why I'm here at 2 AM with this essay.

jokes.text: I'm not allowed to date students. Even though you're an eight. Which is a british ten, so i'm angry.
jokes.setup: Duncan responding to Annie's pitch
jokes.punchline: Completely misinterprets her request as romantic, then explains British vs American rating scales
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy showing Duncan's inappropriate boundary issues and his need to explain the British rating system conversion. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Oh, Annie. I'm flattered, but I'm afraid I must decline. You see, in Britain, we have standards.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: A PG rating here means something entirely different than in America. What you're suggesting would be at least a 15 certificate.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Besides, you're a student, and I'm a professor. That's simply not done across the pond.

jokes.text: I seem to have left my purse in my duffle and my duffle in the boot of my lorry
jokes.setup: Duncan asking Annie for money
jokes.punchline: Overly British terminology makes simple request pretentious and confusing
jokes.explanation: Mild character comedy playing on Duncan's affected British speech patterns. The joke works but isn't particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Miss Edison, I find myself in a rather precarious financial position. Might I entreat you for a modest pecuniary accommodation? Perhaps you could see fit to provide me with a small loan, or as we say in the Commonwealth, a spot of readies to tide me over until my next disbursement of remuneration?

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Professor Duncan, I have no idea what you just said.

jokes.text: No, no, no, no, no. This is my cell phone headset. And this is ear-noculars. It's for spies and whatnot. It gives you sonic hearing.
jokes.setup: Pierce showing off his gadgets
jokes.punchline: Made-up word 'ear-noculars' and belief it gives spy powers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce moment - the made-up word and his earnest belief in spy gadgets fits his delusional character. 'Ear-noculars' is memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: These are ear-noculars. They're like binoculars, but for your ears.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: With these, I can hear conversations from across the room. It's like having spy powers.

jokes.text: All hearing is sonic.
jokes.setup: Pierce claiming sonic hearing
jokes.punchline: Abed's matter-of-fact correction of Pierce's pseudo-science
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed moment - his literal, logical response to Pierce's nonsense. Great economy and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's not how hearing works, Pierce. Sonic refers to the speed of sound, not the ability to hear it. You're just being annoying.

jokes.text: Do they do stuff to your butt? Do you get paid more if they do stuff to your butt?
jokes.setup: Annie asking for psychology experiment volunteers
jokes.punchline: Troy's primary concerns about the experiment
jokes.explanation: Very Troy - his innocent, childlike concerns about the experiment. The escalation from concern to financial opportunity is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Wait, is this gonna hurt? Because I have a very low pain threshold. I cried during that insurance commercial.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: It's just a psychology experiment, Troy.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Yeah, but what if you mess with my brain and I forget how to eat?

jokes.text: They're showing all four indiana joneses at the vista. I'm really looking forward to the first three. I bought a whip.
jokes.setup: Abed explaining why he can't do the experiment
jokes.punchline: Subtle dig at Indiana Jones 4 while revealing he bought a whip
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed - the casual film criticism combined with the matter-of-fact whip purchase. Shows his film knowledge and slight obsessiveness.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I can't do the experiment. I bought a whip.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I know what you're thinking: 'Abed, why would you buy a whip?' Well, I saw Kingdom of the Crystal Skull, and I realized that Indiana Jones movies don't need to be good to make me want to be Indiana Jones.

jokes.text: I figured we were more like chandler and phoebe. They never really had stories together.
jokes.setup: Annie calling them really good friends
jokes.punchline: Analyzes their friendship through Friends character dynamics
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary that's perfectly Abed. Using sitcom analysis to understand real relationships while being in a sitcom himself. Very Community.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: You guys are really good friends.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: We're like Friends. I'm Chandler, Troy's Monica, Shirley's Phoebe, Jeff's Ross, and Britta's Rachel.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Why am I Rachel?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Because you're the least crucial to the group.

jokes.text: Hope he doesn't try to 'handsome' her to death.
jokes.setup: Looking at Vaughn talking to Britta
jokes.punchline: Uses 'handsome' as a verb meaning to charm aggressively
jokes.explanation: Creative wordplay that reveals Jeff's jealousy while being cleverly constructed. The verb usage of 'handsome' is original.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: He's handsoming her.

jokes.text: Oh, no, i was just gonna see if britta-- Hey, no judgment, bro. I used to do coffee.
jokes.setup: Jeff trying to order coffee
jokes.punchline: Vaughn treats coffee like a recovered addiction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Vaughn characterization - treating mundane things with recovery language. The casual 'bro' and 'used to do coffee' establishes his hippie persona.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Can I just get a coffee?

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: Oh man, I can't do that. Coffee's a drug, dude. I'm three years sober from caffeine.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: It's just coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: Just coffee? That's what they all say. One cup leads to two cups, then you're mainlining espresso shots in gas station bathrooms.

jokes.text: Up top, sugar bear.
jokes.setup: Vaughn and Jeff bonding over green tea
jokes.punchline: Overly familiar pet name for someone he just met
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe moment showing Vaughn's boundary issues. The pet name 'sugar bear' is appropriately awkward for someone Jeff just met.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: You know, Jeff, I really appreciate you taking the time to share this green tea with me, buddy.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it's... nice.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: I can tell we're really bonding here, chief. This is special, pal.

jokes.text: And annie... It's never actually going to start.
jokes.setup: Duncan sending Annie to tell subjects they'll start in 5 minutes
jokes.punchline: Reveals the cruel deception to the audience
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony setup - audience knows what subjects don't. Duncan's sadistic pleasure in the deception fits his character.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Annie, go tell them we'll start in five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Okay, Professor Duncan.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: There are no subjects. There is no study. I just wanted to see if she'd do whatever I asked.

jokes.text: No, no, no! No, no, no, no, no! It's unacceptable, all right? You lied to me! When you say something starts at 9:00, it starts at nueve!
jokes.setup: Annie saying they're running behind
jokes.punchline: Chang's explosive reaction ending with Spanish translation of 'nine'
jokes.explanation: Classic Chang explosion with his signature Spanish teacher quirk. The 'nueve' ending is perfectly character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: We're running behind.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: Running behind?! Do you know what time it is?! This is unacceptable! We were supposed to be there an hour ago! An hour! That's like... nine in Spanish!

jokes.text: Houston, we have an idiot.
jokes.setup: Chang's meltdown
jokes.punchline: Parodies 'Houston, we have a problem' for Chang's tantrum
jokes.explanation: Solid reference joke that works in context. Not particularly original but serviceable and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: Houston, we have a problem!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: That's not even close to what's happening right now.

jokes.text: Don't go too fast for these dogs. Short legs.
jokes.setup: Shirley agreeing to walk with Jeff
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecating reference to her height
jokes.explanation: Mild self-deprecating humor that fits Shirley's character but isn't particularly strong or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: I'd love to walk with you, Jeff. But I have to warn you — I walk really slow because my legs are so short.

jokes.text: I know a lot of grown men with radar dishes coming out of their ear.
jokes.setup: Jeff asking if Pierce is right in the head
jokes.punchline: Shirley's sarcastic defense of Pierce's normalcy
jokes.explanation: Good sarcasm that shows Shirley's awareness of Pierce's oddness while maintaining group loyalty. The 'radar dishes' visual is effective.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, Pierce is totally normal. He's just a regular guy who happens to think that yogurt is a government conspiracy and that his skin condition is caused by witches.

jokes.text: Yeah, go, go, gadget incontinence.
jokes.setup: Shirley's Inspector Gadget comparison
jokes.punchline: Extends the gadget theme to Pierce's age-related issues
jokes.explanation: Clever callback that extends the Inspector Gadget metaphor to Pierce's elderly issues. Good wordplay with the catchphrase format.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Pierce, you're like Inspector Gadget, except all your gadgets are just different ways your body is falling apart.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Go go gadget hip replacement!

jokes.text: It's perpetually moist.
jokes.setup: Jeff asking about Vaughn's hair
jokes.punchline: Perfectly gross description of his hair
jokes.explanation: Perfectly disgusting and specific observation. 'Perpetually moist' is an immediately visceral and memorable phrase.

jokes.text: Look at the little one chase the big one. Look at him go. I'm gonna make him go quicker. It's like benny hill looks.
jokes.setup: Duncan watching the subjects
jokes.punchline: Treats human distress like slapstick entertainment
jokes.explanation: Shows Duncan's sadistic enjoyment of the experiment. The Benny Hill reference works but the joke is more about character than being funny itself.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: This is fascinating. Look at the way Subject 7 is experiencing acute psychological distress. The way he's flailing about, the desperation in his voice... it's like watching a perfectly timed pratfall.

jokes.text: The soul train awards were tonight. You promised butt stuff!
jokes.setup: Troy's breakdown after waiting all day
jokes.punchline: His specific grievances escalate absurdly
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from mundane (Soul Train Awards) to absurd (butt stuff promise). Very quotable and fits Troy's childlike priorities perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I've been waiting here for SEVEN HOURS. Seven hours! Do you know what I could've been doing? Studying! Sleeping! Literally anything but standing here like a idiot!

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: And THEN—THEN!—someone ate my yogurt. MY yogurt. The one I labeled. With my name on it. In permanent marker!

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: But that's not even the worst part. The WORST part is that I'm pretty sure it was the MANGO yogurt. Who steals mango? MANGO?! That's the worst flavor!

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: And now my shirt has a wrinkle in it. A WRINKLE. One wrinkle, and it's permanent. I'm going to have to throw out this entire shirt. This is a $40 shirt!

jokes.text: Two cute white people, going to school together, just seems right.
jokes.setup: Shirley wondering why Jeff and Britta aren't together
jokes.punchline: Reduces their compatibility to race and cuteness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Shirley's straightforward, slightly inappropriate observations about race and relationships.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Why aren't you two together? You're both so attractive.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Well, Shirley, it's a little more complicated than that.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: No it's not. You're white, she's cute. Done.

jokes.text: Well, you guys should hang out sometime.
jokes.setup: Jeff saying Vaughn seems cool
jokes.punchline: Britta's suggestion creates exactly what Jeff was trying to avoid
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment - Jeff's attempt to be supportive backfires immediately. The discomfort is palpable.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Vaughn seems cool.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: He is! He's really into yogurt and the environment.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Oh no.

jokes.text: Did you see that hacky sack wears an anklet?
jokes.setup: Continuing gossip about Vaughn
jokes.punchline: Another emasculating detail about Vaughn
jokes.explanation: Continues the pattern of emasculating observations about Vaughn. Works for Shirley's gossip nature but isn't particularly strong.

jokes.text: I'm gonna show her that i am not the jerk she thinks i am and friend the hell out of that green-tea-drinking drum circler.
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining his strategy with Vaughn
jokes.punchline: Using 'friend' as an aggressive verb and detailed description of Vaughn
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Jeff's competitive approach to friendship. 'Friend the hell out of' is clever wordplay, and the description is perfectly dismissive.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm going to friend you so hard, Vaughn. I'm going to friend you with the power of a thousand suns. I'm going to friend you until you don't even know what friending means anymore.

jokes.text: My kids have got hamsters with bigger nips.
jokes.setup: Discussing Vaughn's small nipples
jokes.punchline: Comparison to her children's hamsters
jokes.explanation: Unexpected and specific comparison that's both absurd and vivid. The hamster comparison is perfectly random and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: His nipples are smaller than my children's hamsters.

jokes.text: Sorry you've been waiting 26 hours. It's just gonna be another five minutes.
jokes.setup: The endless psychology experiment
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions it's been 26 hours but still claims 5 more minutes
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - the casual mention of 26 hours followed by the same 5-minute promise shows the absurd persistence of the lie.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: It's been 26 hours.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Just five more minutes.

jokes.text: Go kill john lennon again, you loser.
jokes.setup: Duncan frustrated with his failed experiment
jokes.punchline: Compares Annie to Mark David Chapman
jokes.explanation: Shockingly dark and specific insult that reveals Duncan's cruel nature. The John Lennon reference is unexpectedly harsh and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: You know what? You're like Mark David Chapman.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: What? Why would you say that?

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Because you just shot my experiment dead.

jokes.text: He has warped the duncan principle. Damn you, you outlying piece of datum!
jokes.setup: Duncan's experiment being ruined by Abed
jokes.punchline: Treats Abed as a statistical anomaly using scientific terminology
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Duncan's academic frustration. 'Outlying piece of datum' is perfectly pretentious and specific to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Abed, you've contaminated my entire experiment! Do you understand what you've done?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I was just trying to help.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Help? You're a statistical anomaly. A complete outlier. In fact, I'm going to have to recalibrate my entire methodology because of you. You fall so far outside the normal distribution, you're essentially a data point that invalidates the entire dataset.

jokes.text: Miss annie fanny panties in a bunch!
jokes.setup: Duncan's complete breakdown
jokes.punchline: Childish insult that rhymes with Annie's name
jokes.explanation: Weak wordplay that shows Duncan's infantile breakdown. The joke is more about character degradation than being actually funny.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: You're a... you're a... FANNY!

jokes.text: [Blows raspberry]
jokes.setup: Duncan's complete professional breakdown
jokes.punchline: Responds to question with literal childish sound
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy showing Duncan's complete regression to childish behavior. Works for character but isn't a strong joke itself.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Duncan
dialogue.line: Blblblblbl

jokes.text: Ultimate frisbee at the senior's center. It is amazing.
jokes.setup: Jeff asking about Vaughn's community service
jokes.punchline: Treating elderly frisbee as profound experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Vaughn characterization - finding deep meaning in mundane activities. The enthusiasm makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So how's the community service going?

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: Oh man, it's been a really profound experience. I've been teaching elderly people frisbee, and it's like, they've lived all these years without knowing the beauty of the disc, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: It's frisbee, Vaughn.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: Exactly. And now they know.

jokes.text: Aw, man, if i had a nickel for every time i wished somebody asked me that.
jokes.setup: Jeff asking what makes frisbee ultimate
jokes.punchline: Confusing version of common saying about being asked the question
jokes.explanation: Shows Vaughn's confused but earnest speech patterns. The mangled idiom fits his character well.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What makes frisbee ultimate?

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: Well, that's like asking what makes Ultimate Frisbee ultimate. It's the ultimate of frisbee.

jokes.text: You know, people underestimate the elderly, you know, but you see these guys out there and they're throwing the bee and they're just loving life.
jokes.setup: Vaughn explaining ultimate frisbee
jokes.punchline: Calls frisbee 'the bee' and treats elderly activity as profound
jokes.explanation: Good Vaughn moment with 'throwing the bee' being a memorable phrase. His earnest appreciation of elderly frisbee is appropriately naive.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: It's called 'the bee' because it flies, man. Ultimate frisbee isn't just a sport—it's a way of life. It's about freedom, about soaring above the constraints of society.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: My grandfather plays shuffleboard, and you know what? That's profound too. We're all just trying to find meaning in the flight of our projectiles.

jokes.text: ¿Donde vaughn ustedes los sábados? Domingo, pl-- Oh, okay
jokes.setup: Chang trying to speak Spanish to Vaughn
jokes.punchline: Mangled Spanish followed by Vaughn's confused response
jokes.explanation: Shows Chang's poor Spanish skills despite being a teacher. The confusion is appropriately awkward but not particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: ¿Habla usted español? Mi amigo, yo tengo... uh... los pantalones con queso.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: Dude, I think you just told me you have pants with cheese.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: Sí, sí, very good! Your Spanish is... wait, no.

jokes.text: Did you ever notice where the ocean meets the sky, did ya? It's the same wizard blue that i see in your eye, brit-ta.
jokes.setup: Jeff reading Vaughn's poem aloud
jokes.punchline: Terrible poetry with 'wizard blue' and forced rhyme
jokes.explanation: Perfectly awful poetry that's memorable for all the wrong reasons. 'Wizard blue' became iconic, and the forced pronunciation of 'Brit-ta' is perfectly cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, um... 'Wizard blue, wizard true, wizard me, wizard you.' This doesn't even rhyme!

jokes.text: That is the stain of a teardrop over the word 'rad.'
jokes.setup: Examining Vaughn's poem
jokes.punchline: Identifies emotional evidence on the terrible poem
jokes.explanation: Great forensic observation that makes the poem even more pathetic. The detail about crying over 'rad' is perfectly specific and sad.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, so Vaughn wrote this poem, and it's... it's really bad. Like, objectively terrible. But here's the thing - there are actual tear stains on the paper. So either Vaughn cried while writing this, or he cried while reading it, which somehow makes it worse.

jokes.text: Because you asked me to stay and you said we were friends.
jokes.setup: Asked why he didn't leave the experiment
jokes.punchline: Abed's simple, loyal reasoning
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Abed - his literal interpretation of friendship is both touching and funny. Shows his unique social processing.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I couldn't leave. You guys are my friends.

jokes.text: Making fun of all of us. I heard you with my own ear...noculars.
jokes.setup: Pierce confronting the group about gossip
jokes.punchline: References his spy gadget from earlier
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Pierce's gadget obsession. The pause before 'noculars' shows he remembers his made-up word.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I know you've all been talking about me behind my back. I have proof.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I planted a listening device in the study room. It's disguised as a decorative pen. Cost me $400.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Pierce, that's insane. You can't just spy on us.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Oh, but I can. And I did. For three weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What did you hear?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Everything. And frankly, I'm hurt. You all said my stories are boring.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Well, joke's on you. That pen also records in HD. I'm selling the footage to a reality TV producer.

jokes.text: Calling me 'hacky sack' and ripping my--my six pack abs and my moist hair.
jokes.setup: Pierce listing the gossip he overheard
jokes.punchline: Claims to have six-pack abs and describes his hair as moist
jokes.explanation: Pierce's delusional self-image conflicts with reality. The 'six-pack abs' claim is particularly absurd for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: He claims to have six-pack abs and describes his hair as moist.

jokes.text: Maybe he has a tiny penis.
jokes.setup: Pierce joining the gossip about Vaughn
jokes.punchline: Immediately suggests the most inappropriate detail
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce moment - immediately escalating to the most inappropriate suggestion. Shows his lack of social boundaries.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Is he the guy with the weird thing?

jokes.text: Everything's all, 'no worries, no worries.' And he always says hello three times in a row. Hello, good evening, how are you?
jokes.setup: Jeff describing Vaughn's speech patterns
jokes.punchline: Specific observation about his repetitive greetings
jokes.explanation: Good specific observation about Vaughn's conversational tics. The triple greeting example is accurate and annoying.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Vaughn has this thing where he says 'hey' at the beginning and end of every sentence. 'Hey, how are you? Hey.' It's like verbal bookends.

jokes.text: Hey. What's up? Hi.
jokes.setup: Jeff just criticized Vaughn's triple greetings
jokes.punchline: Vaughn immediately demonstrates the exact behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and structure - Vaughn proves Jeff's point exactly as described. The immediate payoff is comedy gold.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: Hey! Hey! Hey! What's up, Jeff? How are you doing? How are you feeling? How are you?

jokes.text: This troop's got a case of the giggles, huh?
jokes.setup: Everyone laughing at him
jokes.punchline: Vaughn's oblivious, childish description of mockery
jokes.explanation: Perfect Vaughn obliviousness. 'Case of the giggles' is appropriately childish and shows his inability to read the room.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: What? Why is everyone laughing? Did I say something funny? I was just trying to be helpful. You guys are being really mean right now. This is like that time in third grade when everyone made fun of my lunch box because it had my name on it in puffy paint. I worked really hard on that puffy paint.

jokes.text: This is the least tight thing that's ever happened to me.
jokes.setup: Vaughn realizing they're mocking him
jokes.punchline: Uses his own slang to describe emotional pain
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment where Vaughn's slang becomes poignant. The juxtaposition of his casual language with real hurt is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Vaughn
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Are you guys making fun of me right now? Because that's not cool, man. That's not cool at all. You know what? This hurts. This really hurts. And I'm gonna describe it using my own slang because that's how I express myself: Dude, you just gave me a major bummer-downer.

jokes.text: Oh, jeff. I have a gossip problem. I stir the pot, jeff. I'm a pot stirrer.
jokes.setup: Shirley confessing her role in the drama
jokes.punchline: Clinical self-diagnosis of her gossip addiction
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Shirley's self-awareness about her gossiping. The repetition of 'pot stirrer' emphasizes her compulsiveness.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: I have a problem. I'm addicted to gossip. I can't help myself. I see someone and I just have to tell everyone their business.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: My therapist says it's a control issue, but what does she know? She's just sitting there listening to me all day.

jokes.text: They call me 'tattle-ina.' It's a bumblebee nickname. It's--it's cute, but it stings.
jokes.setup: Shirley explaining her reputation
jokes.punchline: Plays on bumblebee imagery with 'cute but it stings'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting the bee-themed nickname to actual stinging. Shows Shirley's awareness of how her gossip hurts people.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: I know what people say about me. They think I'm all sweetness and light, like a cute little bumblebee. But let me tell you something—bumblebees sting. And I will sting you.

jokes.text: What are we gonna talk about? My kids? Your--your doctor career?
jokes.setup: Shirley wondering what they'll discuss without gossip
jokes.punchline: Realizes they have nothing in common beyond gossip
jokes.explanation: Good observation about how gossip was the foundation of their friendship. Shows the emptiness underneath their connection.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: What are we gonna talk about if we don't have gossip?

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Oh my God... we have nothing in common.

jokes.text: Britta told me she had a sex dream about you.
jokes.setup: Shirley promising to stop gossiping
jokes.punchline: Immediately shares the biggest gossip possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - immediately breaks her anti-gossip promise with nuclear gossip. The timing and Shirley's inability to resist is perfectly character-driven.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Okay, you know what? I'm done gossiping. From this moment on, I'm a new woman. No more talking about other people's business.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Oh my God, did you know that Dean Pelton has been ordering women's shoes to his office? Size 10!

jokes.text: Together: the fourth one blows.
jokes.setup: Abed explaining why he only bought first three Indiana Jones movies
jokes.punchline: Both characters simultaneously agree about the fourth movie
jokes.explanation: Nice callback showing shared pop culture knowledge. The simultaneous delivery suggests this is universal opinion.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I only bought the first three Indiana Jones movies.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Why not the fourth one?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: It doesn't exist.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: It doesn't exist.

jokes.text: Is there a spot on the friendship spectrum between total stranger and having to hear about the guys you date?
jokes.setup: Jeff trying to define their relationship
jokes.punchline: Treats friendship like a technical specification
jokes.explanation: Great Jeff moment treating friendship like a technical problem. The 'friendship spectrum' concept is both specific and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Look, let's be clear about what this is. We're friends. But I need to establish some parameters here. Friendship isn't some nebulous emotional contract—it's a technical specification. You've got your core friend functions, your optional friend modules, and your friendship-breaking bugs that void the whole agreement.

jokes.text: How about the friend level where you sometimes have to cat-sit?
jokes.setup: Jeff's friendship level negotiation
jokes.punchline: Britta offers a specific, lower-commitment alternative
jokes.explanation: Good response that continues the negotiation metaphor. Cat-sitting is perfectly calibrated as a mid-level friendship duty.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Okay, but what if we were just friends who occasionally hang out and text each other memes?

jokes.text: You see, jeff, there are certain things man was not meant to hear. We were designed, by whatever entity you choose, to hear what's in this range, and really, this range alone.
jokes.setup: Pierce explaining why he got rid of the ear-noculars
jokes.punchline: Pseudo-philosophical explanation about human hearing limitations
jokes.explanation: Good Pierce moment with his pseudo-wisdom. The callback to the gadget works, though the joke is more philosophical than funny.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Look, the human ear wasn't meant to see that far. We're not evolved for it. God gave us ears for a reason — to hear things close by, like our loved ones telling us we're great.

jokes.text: Because you know who's talking to us in this range? The people we love.
jokes.setup: Pierce's philosophy about hearing ranges
jokes.punchline: Surprisingly touching conclusion about loved ones
jokes.explanation: Unexpectedly touching Pierce moment that reframes his earlier gadget obsession as wisdom about human connection. Well-executed character growth.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: You know, as you get older, your hearing goes. First the high frequencies disappear, then the lows. Eventually, all you can hear is the mid-range where human voices live. It's like God's way of making sure that no matter how deaf you become, you can still hear the people you love.

jokes.text: [With jamaican accent] Hey, man, coming through. I love de desperate housewives.
jokes.setup: Troy doing impressions in the final tag
jokes.punchline: Random Jamaican accent praising Desperate Housewives
jokes.explanation: Tag scene randomness that's more about Troy's playfulness than strong comedy. The accent and show choice are arbitrary.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Mon, Desperate Housewives is de best show on television, mon!

jokes.text: I'm dr. Doogie seacrest. I think i'm better than everyone else because i'm 40.
jokes.setup: Troy continuing impressions
jokes.punchline: Mashup of Doogie Howser and Ryan Seacrest with age complaint
jokes.explanation: Random celebrity mashup that shows Troy's pop culture knowledge but isn't particularly strong. The age comment adds mild bite.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Doogie Howser, M.D., here. I'm a young prodigy, but I'm also tired all the time. Hi, I'm Ryan Seacrest, and I'm exhausted. I've been doing this since I was 16 years old. My back hurts, my knees are shot. I'm only 26!

jokes.text: I can hear you through the window, morons.
jokes.setup: Troy and Abed doing loud impressions outside
jokes.punchline: Jeff's exasperated response from inside
jokes.explanation: Simple tag ending that shows Jeff's annoyance. Economy works but it's not a strong closer.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Can you guys keep it down? Some of us are trying to enjoy our narcissism in peace!

tmdb_overview: As Annie recruits Abed and Troy for a psychology class experiment, Jeff and Shirley bond over their mutual fondness for gossip.
insight: Pierce's casual racism hits hardest here: character comedy drives 38 of 66 jokes.
